sentences.text: Yeah.
I actually-- it was a Jewish jail, [INAUDIBLE], but it was a jail.
We had-- we had counting.
What-- what do you call it?
Every day and morning, in the evening.
Appeal, yeah-- appell.
We had appellplatz.
And-- and it happened also one-- one day, this guy, SS man, Pilaczek, is his name-- Pilaczek.
He was in-- in the ghetto-- [NON-ENGLISH] and Pilaczek.
All of a sudden, he brought in a young guy with a beard, a black beard, with-- with glasses.
Just-- just for entertainment, he says, run.
So here-- and then we were on the appell-- on the appellplatz-- whole-- so he's told-- told him to-- to run.
He took out the gun, and he started-- 1, 2, 3, 4.
And he fell down.
And he started with a big smile, and he left out.
This is-- this is the way it was-- Pilaczek.
Now he was SS, Pilaczek?
He was SS.
And this-- this was the jail where we were there.
We were under the Jewish control, but-- under the Jewish hands, but under German control.
And this happened-- this went through New Year's, till '43.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Two or three months-- two-- two and 1/2 months, I was there.
And before I was there-- before I was there, before they took me in, they took us into a bath, you know, in Cracow, in ghetto.
And my sister came out with a-- with a young baby-- with the baby, and brought me a package.
You know?
Before-- oh, no, no.
This was later.
After that, they assembled us in the-- in-- in the-- in the holdings things-- in the jail.
And they took us to a bath.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my sister was waiting there and gave me a package.
She-- she-- she herself was in trouble, with a-- with a-- with a six-month-old baby.
And she gave me this.
I told her to-- and I took it from her.
And they loaded us on-- on the train.
And they-- they brought also-- they brought-- they emptied out also, in Cracow-- it was a main jail, which was called-- a criminal jail.
What was it called?
Montelupich-- Montelupich in Cracow.
They emptied out the jail in Montelupich, and they made a transport-- us with them.
So these were Jews and non-Jews in the jail in Cracow?
Criminals, yeah.
Criminals.
They emptied out the jail, and they send us on the railroad.
Stayed in jail.
Stayed in jail.
And this was the end.
The end was this here.
It was in Cracow, as I said, in January 19.
They shipped us.
And I'm familiar, because I-- I saw the way going.
It went-- went-- went towards my town.
And we learned that in Auschwitz.
I saw already the lights, with the-- with the-- with the-- with the-- what do you call it?
Where-- by the-- where the guards were there on the top of the-- and we landed there.
It was already dark.
It was winter-- 6 o'clock or 8 o'clock-- 6 o'clock and-- and-- so-- How big was the transport on the train?
How big?
How far?
Not trains.
You mean-- you mean cars.
Cattle cars.
Cattle cars.
I suppose there are a few hundred.
And from there, it was more.
So it was-- it was-- it was a transport.
I didn't count how many, but-- but they-- they took us-- they-- they took us to Auschwitz.
Coming to Auschwitz, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] when they unloaded us, I didn't even-- even know what I saw.
I saw SS-- SS there.
And it was-- it was strange to me.
This whole-- the whole scenery was strange to me.
I didn't-- I-- but I-- I wasn't used to it.
And asking questions-- you go here, go here, go here, go here.
But I didn't notice it.
I was-- I was too busy, too wild.
All-- suddenly, I should know what's going on.
I didn't even know what's going on.
There they took us into the camp-- 'Arbeit macht frei.' This was-- this was this-- this-- this thing here, which I-- what I remember that.
And coming into the camp, I saw people walking there.
They are people, but they don't look like people.
They look skin and bone-- skin and bone.
And we had-- everybody had to have something, a rucksack, whatever.
We had a package.
So they-- they-- they jumped.
They jumped to-- to-- to tear off this package.
And while they jumped, we heard, with sticks-- with-- with hard sticks, knocking on the heads.
That they-- the-- the head-- the-- the head was-- was-- was-- was-- was going in 1/2.
But they still-- like-- like-- like a fly goes to the fire.
They know they're gonna get burned, but that's how they run.
And this is what I saw.
This-- this scenery, I can never forget.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was Auschwitz in-- in January '93.
So they brought us to a block and call it a hotel.
Did they divide you at that time when you get off the train-- some people to one side, some people to another side?
They divided.
They divided.
But I didn't notice even it.
They must have divided it.
Because we were only Jews.
And they-- they were-- they were the-- the Polacks.
So they did-- they somehow, maybe-- maybe-- maybe they loaded us in separate wagons.
But we-- we were from the Jews which came up to there.
And they send us to a block.
To a block first.
This block, [SIGHS] I don't think-- this block was a quarantine block.
I don't even remember.
I-- I don't even remember the block-- the number.
It's like-- it was a quarantine block.
When we came to this block, [SIGHS] we were busy till late in the night.
Because they took us first to a [NON-ENGLISH], where they gave us the numbers.
Everybody had to go through.
They gave us the numbers.
And this is the number I got now.
I think I told you-- 90124.
This is the number I got there.
So in other words, you give up your name.
And you bought a number.
And the number is worth millions.
You would-- the number must be.
The name doesn't matter.
The-- the people that-- the-- the-- the men doesn't have to be.
Because you-- we are-- you are killed anyway.
But the number must be, because they have to have a count.
This was the numbers.
You-- you weren't a-- a people, you were a number.
This is what it was.
So [SIGHS] I was there.
And 5 o'clock in the morning, out.
We came there [LAUGHS] 1 o'clock, 2 o'clock.
They take a whole night.
But for 5 o'clock, we had to be out with a bitter frost, a bitter cold.
I had already a number.
And they gave us coffee.
You know coffee?
There was no chocolate in the coffee.
[LAUGHS]
So I looked around, and I saw going out-- in the morning, I saw people outside the block-- twisted, winded.
So I asked, what it is.
What is this?
They said, those are Muselmanns-- Muselmanns.
You know, if they go to the toilet, they don't come back.
So they stay there outside.
And every day was the same.
So I ask there-- someday I ask there, tell me, how long do you got to live here to-- in order to survive?
I saw-- I saw more-- more or less he-- he-- he was-- if you will survive, you will see.
This is what the answer.
If-- if you will be here this long, you will see.
This was the answer.
That's when we-- we's-- when we stood there for a few days.
And then did-- on a-- in a few days, I saw this guy-- you know, he-- he was from Cracow.
I know him-- Hacker.
You know, I-- I don't remember the first name.
He was in-- in Auschwitz in a-- a 3/4 coat, you know, with-- with boots.
And he went-- went like-- like-- like the sidewalk was shaking.
And I saw him a few days later.
I saw already the nose was blue, and it was running-- and running dripping [INAUDIBLE].
I-- he says to me, this is the way it is.
You-- I realized what-- what-- where I am.
All of a sudden-- I was with a cousin here.
I told you that I-- I-- I went-- in Cracow, I went-- I couldn't be by my sister, and I went to my-- with my cousin.
And he had a-- we were five in-- in his-- the cousin was there too.
And he was-- he was a Cracowian.
He was born here.
They took his parents away from Cracow.
So he was in Cracow.
So he joined a few of his friends in the ghetto, and I joined them.
Moniek Fuchsbrunner-- Fuchsbrunner, my name.
His father was a cousin to my father.
So he was-- he was-- he was before the war, he was a mechanic.
A [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what it means, [NON-ENGLISH]?
A mechanic.
He good in-- in-- in mechanics.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So I was with him together there.
All of a sudden, the [NON-ENGLISH] comes.
You know what a [NON-ENGLISH] is?
The [NON-ENGLISH], from the block.

They are looking for hand craft, like carpenters, and-- and-- and-- and, [NON-ENGLISH] and-- and the other things in there, you know, more-- more or less the-- the-- the brick work, they're looking for.
So my cousin told me, I'm gonna go.
I'm a [NON-ENGLISH], so I have a chance.
It can't be-- can't be-- can't be worse than here.
Here, I can see every-- every day, every day is-- is nearer-- nearer to the end.
So he went.
So I said, I'm gonna go too with you.
And this is the way I stuck with him.
And they lined us up.
And they took 25 young people.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then, from the whole group there-- 25-- and from there, they took us-- they took us to the bath.
And they gave us the stripes.
And they brought us back.
So everybody was looking at us.
How did you do it?
How did you do this?
You know, the-- they-- they were-- there was the envy.
I don't know why.
[LAUGHS] It like-- like-- like I would make the-- the-- the-- the lottery, you know?
That's what it was.
And they brought us a little kettle-- you know, a kettle of-- of-- of soup, which, this was a rarity.
This was millions.
You couldn't buy it.
For us, 25, do you know what it means?
And the other ones were looking.
They were looking, and they gave us a-- a- a plate.
[NON-ENGLISH].
You know what it means?
And from there, they took us on a-- on a-- on a-- on a-- a truck.
They took us out from there-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and to a-- to another-- [LAUGHS] to-- to another camp-- to another thing.
And there again, they en-loused us again.
You know what it means, en-louse, yeah?
Same thing what was in Birkenau-- they took us to Auschwitz.
They en-loused us again, and they brought us in to block 11 in Auschwitz.
I didn't know what it is-- what block 11 is.
They took us to block 11.
Over there, they let us-- let us in with the-- with SS man in front at the door.
You know what I mean?
You couldn't-- in the other-- the other blocks, it was open.
But this was closed.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] coming in there, they took us to the basement.
And over there in the basement was already a-- a group.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So we joined them-- coffee, whatever. 5 o'clock in the morning, next morning, they woke us up.
And they walked us out in the morning-- dark, no appeal-- no appell, no nothing.
They walked us.
A guard walk-- two guards or one guard, walked us out of the camp, from the [NON-ENGLISH], where we came in.
And a short walk, we saw a building standing there with a-- with-- with a-- with a wood-- with a fence, a-- a wooden fence or a hard fence.
And they opened up the gate.
And they-- and they brought us in.
Over there, we met [SIGHS] a young guy, a-- a-- a Polack.
His name was Mietek Morawa.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He's supposed to be kapo.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And on-- on-- on a, [SIGHS] you know, since then-- and the SS man comes in.
He makes a little bit of a speech, now, that this-- this is work.
And if you work, you'll live.
And this is the end of it.
And right away, after that, this Mietek took a-- a-- a-- a-- a-- a hard club.
He start clobbering.
We went outside.
He took us outside.
Outside, this was the crematorium.
He took us outside in-- in-- in the-- in the-- in the-- the-- what-- what do you call it in-- in the backyard-- in the front yard, where by the-- by the gate was there.
In dark, it was.
And I saw a pile-- a piled up frozen.
And I didn't know even what it is.
It was dark.
And then he came out with a club.
He says, pull them in!
Pull them in!
We couldn't even start.
It-- this was Muselmanns-- dead ones.
But they were frozen.
Because there was water on top of it.
The first was-- was cold.
We had to-- you had-- we had to part them.
We had to-- we-- we-- we-- we exhausted doing this.
And he was running.
And he was running after us.
And we brought them in.
And then the-- the-- the job was-- there was ovens.
And it was a-- a truck which goes on the wheels.
You put up a few bodies, and you push 'em in.
They learn you how to do it-- and go into that oven.
And the one that was holding the bodies-- and they pulled out the oven.
Anyway, we stood there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two weeks, maybe three weeks.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We came 25.
We came back 10 or 11.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The rest of it didn't make it-- didn't make it.
And I was--
First of all, we had to bring 'em in.
[SIGHS] We had to bring-- because the truck-- when the truck came at night, he lifted them up.
And the bodies fell down, and they were laying there for-- for the night.
And they were frozen to one piece.
We had to separate them.
And it wasn't-- it wasn't easy to separate them.
And he was behind us with clubs.
No, with the hands.
Mietek Morawa-- this-- this was a young guy, a Polack, a blond one, maybe-- maybe 22 years.
And he eliminated us to 1/2 the size.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
No, we-- we took these bodies in.
It was a-- a mortuary, a big room.
We brought them in.
From there, then, we took in one by one to the-- to the ovens.
It was there.
It-- there was-- there was a-- a small crematorium with a-- a big chimney.
There was no-- was-- there was no method.
There was no method-- bringing bodies, and-- and that's it-- that's all.
It was-- was placed, put in-- in another one.
It should be full.
But usually, the-- the-- this we learned.
This-- this-- how you should divide it, we learned it in-- in Birkenau, which I-- I'll come later to it.
But anyway, he rushed us.
We had to bring in the bodies and-- and load it on the-- on the truck.
And the truck was on rails.
You know, on rails?
On the oven was two rails.
And the truck was-- was like a-- a-- a moving truck with-- with wheels.
And we moved it in.
And somebody hold it back with-- with a fork in order for them to-- to stay there.
And he pulled-- pulled back.
It was a team.
And-- and we did this for a few weeks.
Then, in doing it later, in the regular hours, came the original team, which was there-- one, the Slovaks.
One was a Frenchman.
One was from Warsaw.
And one was-- and-- and they came into-- to work.
But we'll-- we had to learn the work, you know?
They showed us the work.
What it means, showing-- not showing-- what do we need-- what do you need us?
If-- if you are here, or what do we need here?
There was no way to knowing until [SIGHS] January and middle of February.
We were there.
Took us back to Auschwitz-- to Birkenau.
At Birkenau, they took us to block-- it was block two-- old Birkenau, you see, with-- beginning, in like-- this was in January '93.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] '43.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And so they brought us into this block.
And in this block was already a full block with Jews.
And they called itself Sonderkommando.
They brought us in Sonderkommando.
And I learned there are a lot of things.
What was-- what-- what-- what-- what it was about-- I saw already.
In Auschwitz was more or less-- later, what I found out for Muselmann, you know, all the people died.
And specials-- you know what I mean?
This was specials.
Because this was all-- this was like-- like-- like you serve after the meal.
What do you-- what do you call it here?
Huh?
You-- you know?
This-- this was a special-- little things-- 100, 150, 70-- Some of them to-- to-- to shoot or maybe sometime they-- they wanted to save-- to save the gas, and they shoot.
Anyway, they-- when they brought us to Auschwitz, to Birkenau, I found out where I am.
They put us in a block two, to Sonderkommando.
Oh, that's where I am.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And [SIGHS] what are you doing?
They are-- they are going to bunkers.
They have-- they had two shifts, three shifts, two-- bunker one, bunker two, bunker three.
Bunkers were-- what do you call it-- caves-- where they brought transports out of Birkenau.
And there was a-- what do you call it-- a-- from a-- a-- the-- the-- the little-- the-- the-- Poland.
You know what mean, Poland?
They-- they lived in-- in-- in the little town there.
They emptied out the whole-- the whole the-- the place, and they-- and they fixed there a house for a-- for-- for-- for gassing.
So they brought them in the winter, or in the summer, whenever it was.
They undressed.
In one house, they undressed.
And they put in the bunker.
And then they gassed them.
And from the gassing, there was no ovens.
So the Sonderkommando had to make the-- the-- the bunkers, the-- the-- the-- the-- the holes.
They had a way of-- of putting two by fours back, and forth, like this, like this.
And they-- they-- then-- then they had to saturate with gasoline or whatever-- or whatever.
I didn't work there.
But the commander worked there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So that's where I was.
What do you-- what do you need me?
I-- if you have here-- I-- if you have hundreds of people.
And then I learned they were there.
They came from Bialystok, from Lomza.
They came from-- from-- Markowa, and from other towns there-- there-- from Grodno.
This transport came by the end of the year.
So they filled up-- where we-- I came in.
They stood there already for two months.
Why-- why-- why did they stay for two months?
Before then, there was a commander, which they killed.
They gassed them all.
They killed them all off, so they took a new commander.
You know?
Some-- sometime-- something I heard, that [SIGHS] they planned something.
They didn't plan something.
Whatever it was-- reasons-- there's plenty of reasons for doing this.
Am I-- am I-- am I too far away?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So this was what it was.
This commander was there.
And they came home from the-- from the-- and they changed-- day shift, night shift; bunker one, bunker two; bunker two, bunker one; day and night for shifts, hundreds of people.
They were busy.
All Jews?
All Jews-- only Jews.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So [SIGHS] us they didn't call in.
For us, they make our commander, the 10 or 12 people, whatever it was, and they took us in in Birkenau in a-- in a new building, a crematorium two.
Auschwitz was crematorium one, and this one, they built a big crematorium, crematorium two.
And what they send us to-- to Auschwitz, to learn the trade-- the ovens.
Because the other ones-- we wanted bunkers, which were-- they throw the two-- they-- the-- the-- the people in-- into this here.
Then they-- they had to empty out the-- this, because the-- the-- the earth was-- was-- was-- was-- was-- was turning back with-- with-- with the bodies.
They were swollen in summertime-- big troubles.
So this was the beginning of crematorium.
So they built a big crematorium there.
And we supposed to be the specialists in the bunker-- in other words, how to-- how to run the ovens.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So we-- experience.
So they took us in.
And there was one oberkapo.
The oberkapo's name was August Bruck-- August.
Actually, there were three-- three kind of signs here.
One was a-- a green.
One was a yellow.
One was a red.
The green was criminals.
The yellow, I don't know what it was.
The-- the red was political.
We were political.
We-- we had a red.
You know what I mean?
But he had a green.
So he was a criminal.
So later, we found out he came from-- from Sachsenhausen or from Buchenwald.
But he was a specialized man.
He-- he looked-- he looked at me.
He was almost middle age, skin boned, tall.
And he looked at you.
And he knew what you-- he knew who you are, you know?
He wasn't a bad man all-- altogether.
But I don't know.
He didn't give orders to kill, but he-- he gave his advices how to do it.
They eliminated the trucks with the wheels.
All they had there-- and this-- this in the new building.
There was-- there was a-- 15 in a row, you know? 15 ovens.
They didn't play small.
They-- so-- they-- they played big.
Why, if you have to play, you got to play, right?
So 15 ovens-- and they didn't have no more trucks on-- on-- on-- on rails.
But they had like a-- big plates, plates with two handles.
And it was a little bit like-- like-- like a rounding-- rounded.
And on the end, they had two-- two-- two-- what do you-- what do you call it?
Which-- which-- which the plates were laying.
It was a-- it was a-- Handles?
Not handles, but to move it-- to move it on-- on-- on the--
--ovens was a-- was-- was a-- a-- a trestle, like, like, you know?
To put up the rail, this-- this-- this--
Heh?
Long poles.
But-- but in the middle, was-- was to take a body.
You know what I mean?
Like a-- like a dish.
And it-- it-- it rested on the two rails by the oven-- and then to move it in.
And then it was a-- he gave--
He gave-- he-- he learned how to do it.
Two sides from-- from-- we had a stick.
And-- and-- and-- and the-- the-- the-- the plate was laying on-- on-- on the stick, one stick from-- from front, one stick-- stick in the back.
And in the-- in the-- in the back, all together was-- was two handles.
And when they load-- we loaded the dead bodies, one on the other.
And the one-- then it went on top-- three.
So we-- we--
OK.
Yeah.
